title: Chemical Equilibrium
subtitle: Chapter 8: Chemical Equilibrium — Complete NEB Notes

content.text: 1. Introduction to Equilibrium

Equilibrium: The state of a reversible reaction in which the rate of forward reaction becomes equal to the rate of backward reaction, and there is no change in the concentration of reactants and products with time.
At equilibrium, the reaction appears to stop but actually both forward and backward reactions continue to occur at equal rates. This is called dynamic equilibrium.
2. Types of Equilibrium
1. Physical Equilibrium: Equilibrium established in physical processes (phase changes).
2. Chemical Equilibrium: Equilibrium established in chemical reactions where opposing processes involve chemical changes.
2.1 Physical Equilibrium Examples
Liquid-Vapour Equilibrium: H₂O(l) ⇌ H₂O(g) — Rate of evaporation = Rate of condensation
Solid-Liquid Equilibrium: Ice ⇌ Water at 0°C and 1 atm — Rate of melting = Rate of freezing
Solid-Gas Equilibrium: Sublimation ⇌ Condensation (e.g., I₂(s) ⇌ I₂(g))
Solute-Solution Equilibrium: Dissolution ⇌ Crystallization (e.g., NaCl(s) ⇌ Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq))
3. Reversible and Irreversible Reactions
Reversible Reaction: A reaction that can proceed in both forward and backward directions simultaneously under the same conditions. Represented by double arrow (⇌).
Example: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g)
Irreversible Reaction: A reaction that proceeds in one direction only and goes to completion. Represented by single arrow (→).
Example: AgNO₃(aq) + NaCl(aq) → AgCl(s) + NaNO₃(aq)

content.label: 🔑 Key Differences
content.text: Reversible: Never goes to completion, maintains equilibrium, occurs in closed vessel • Irreversible: Goes to completion, no equilibrium, can occur in open or closed vessel
4. Law of Mass Action

Law of Mass Action (Guldberg and Waage, 1864): The rate of a chemical reaction at a given temperature is directly proportional to the product of the active masses (molar concentrations) of the reactants raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients.
For a reaction: aA + bB → Products

content.name: Rate Expression
content.note: k = rate constant, [A] and [B] = molar concentrations

content.text: Active Mass: Molar concentration expressed in mol/L or M. For gases, it can also be expressed in terms of partial pressure.
5. Equilibrium Constant (Kc)
For a general reversible reaction: aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD

content.name: Equilibrium Constant Expression
content.note: Kc = equilibrium constant in terms of concentration

content.text: At equilibrium: Rate of forward reaction = Rate of backward reaction

content.name: Derivation
content.note: kf = forward rate constant, kb = backward rate constant

content.label: 📌 Important Points
content.text: • Kc is constant at a given temperature • Independent of initial concentrations • Depends only on temperature • Pure solids and liquids are not included (their activity = 1)
6. Equilibrium Constant in Terms of Pressure (Kp)
For gaseous reactions, equilibrium constant can be expressed in terms of partial pressures:

content.name: Kp Expression
content.note: PA, PB, PC, PD = partial pressures of gases A, B, C, D

content.text: 7. Relationship Between Kp and Kc

content.name: Relationship
content.note: R = gas constant (0.0821 L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹), T = temperature in Kelvin, Δn = (c+d) - (a+b) = change in number of moles

content.text: Three Cases:
1. Δn = 0: Kp = Kc (e.g., H₂ + I₂ ⇌ 2HI)
2. Δn > 0: Kp > Kc (e.g., PCl₅ ⇌ PCl₃ + Cl₂, Δn = 1)
3. Δn < 0: Kp < Kc (e.g., N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃, Δn = -2)
8. Characteristics of Equilibrium Constant
1. Constant Value: Kc or Kp has a definite value for a particular reaction at a given temperature.
2. Temperature Dependence: K changes with temperature but is independent of concentration, pressure, or catalyst.
3. Reversing Reaction: If reaction is reversed, K' = 1/K
4. Multiplying Coefficients: If coefficients are multiplied by n, K' = Kⁿ
5. Adding Reactions: If reactions are added, K = K₁ × K₂
6. Magnitude Indicates Extent: Large K (>>1) favors products; Small K (<<1) favors reactants
9. Le Chatelier's Principle

Le Chatelier's Principle (1884): "If a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the equilibrium shifts in that direction which tends to neutralize or counteract the effect of the change."
9.1 Effect of Concentration
Increasing Reactant Concentration: Equilibrium shifts in forward direction (towards products)
Increasing Product Concentration: Equilibrium shifts in backward direction (towards reactants)
9.2 Effect of Temperature
Exothermic Reaction (ΔH < 0): Increasing temperature shifts equilibrium backward (towards reactants)
Endothermic Reaction (ΔH > 0): Increasing temperature shifts equilibrium forward (towards products)
Example: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ + Heat (ΔH = -92 kJ/mol) — Exothermic, so low temperature favors NH₃ formation
9.3 Effect of Pressure
Increasing Pressure: Equilibrium shifts towards the side with fewer moles of gas
Decreasing Pressure: Equilibrium shifts towards the side with more moles of gas
Example: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g) — 4 moles → 2 moles, so high pressure favors forward reaction
Note: Pressure has no effect if Δn = 0 (equal moles on both sides)
9.4 Effect of Catalyst
Catalyst does not affect the position of equilibrium. It only increases the rate of both forward and backward reactions equally, helping to reach equilibrium faster.
10. Industrial Applications of Le Chatelier's Principle
10.1 Haber's Process (Ammonia Synthesis)
Optimum Conditions:
1. Temperature: 723-773 K (450-500°C) — Moderate temperature (compromise between yield and rate)
2. Pressure: 200-300 atm — High pressure favors forward reaction
3. Catalyst: Finely divided iron with molybdenum promoter
4. Concentration: Excess N₂ and H₂
10.2 Contact Process (Sulfuric Acid Manufacture)
Optimum Conditions:
1. Temperature: 723 K (450°C)
2. Pressure: 1.5-2 atm (slightly above atmospheric)
3. Catalyst: V₂O₅ (Vanadium pentoxide) or Pt
10.3 Birkeland-Eyde Process (Nitric Acid)
Conditions:
1. Temperature: 3000°C (very high, as reaction is endothermic)
2. Pressure: No effect (Δn = 0)
3. Electric Arc: Used to produce high temperature
11. Ionic Equilibrium
Ionic Equilibrium: The equilibrium established between unionized molecules and ions in solution of weak electrolytes.
Example: CH₃COOH(aq) ⇌ CH₃COO⁻(aq) + H⁺(aq)
12. Acids and Bases
12.1 Arrhenius Concept
Arrhenius Acid: Substance that gives H⁺ ions in aqueous solution. Example: HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃
Arrhenius Base: Substance that gives OH⁻ ions in aqueous solution. Example: NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂
Limitations: Only applicable to aqueous solutions; cannot explain acidic/basic nature of non-aqueous substances
12.2 Bronsted-Lowry Concept
Bronsted-Lowry Acid: Proton (H⁺) donor
Bronsted-Lowry Base: Proton (H⁺) acceptor
Example: HCl + NH₃ → NH₄⁺ + Cl (HCl donates H⁺, NH₃ accepts H⁺)
Conjugate Acid-Base Pair: Two species that differ by one proton (H⁺).
Example: NH₃ (base) and NH₄⁺ (conjugate acid); HCl (acid) and Cl⁻ (conjugate base)
12.3 Lewis Concept
Lewis Acid: Electron pair acceptor. Examples: BF₃, AlCl₃, Fe³⁺, H⁺
Lewis Base: Electron pair donor. Examples: NH₃, H₂O, OH⁻, Cl⁻
Example: BF₃ + :NH₃ → F₃B:NH₃ (BF₃ accepts electron pair, NH₃ donates)

content.label: 📊 Comparison
content.text: Arrhenius: Limited to aqueous • Bronsted-Lowry: Proton transfer • Lewis: Electron pair transfer (most general)
13. pH and pOH
pH: Negative logarithm (base 10) of hydrogen ion concentration.
pOH: Negative logarithm of hydroxide ion concentration.
pH Scale: Acidic: pH < 7 • Neutral: pH = 7 • Basic: pH > 7

content.label: 💡 Quick Calculation
content.text: If [H⁺] = 10⁻³ M, then pH = 3 • If pH = 5, then [H⁺] = 10⁻⁵ M
14. Buffer Solutions
Buffer Solution: A solution that resists changes in pH upon addition of small amounts of acid or base, or upon dilution.
14.1 Types of Buffers
1. Acidic Buffer: Weak acid + its salt with strong base. Example: CH₃COOH + CH₃COONa (pH ≈ 4.75)
2. Basic Buffer: Weak base + its salt with strong acid. Example: NH₄OH + NH₄Cl (pH ≈ 9.25)
14.2 Buffer Action
When acid is added: Base component neutralizes H⁺ • When base is added: Acid component neutralizes OH⁻
Applications: Blood (pH 7.4), Industrial processes, Laboratory work, Food preservation
15. Solubility Product (Ksp)
Solubility Product (Ksp): The product of molar concentrations of ions in a saturated solution of a sparingly soluble salt at constant temperature.
For a salt: AₓB(s) ⇌ xAʸ⁺(aq) + yBˣ⁻(aq)
Examples:
AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ → Ksp = [Ag⁺][Cl⁻]
CaF₂(s) ⇌ Ca²⁺ + 2F⁻ → Ksp = [Ca²⁺][F⁻]²
Al(OH)₃(s) ⇌ Al³⁺ + 3OH⁻ → Ksp = [Al³⁺][OH⁻]³
15.1 Applications of Ksp
1. Predicting Precipitation: If ionic product > Ksp, precipitation occurs
2. Purification of Common Salt: Passing HCl gas through saturated NaCl solution
3. Qualitative Analysis: Group separation in salt analysis
4. Soap Manufacturing: Adding NaCl to precipitate soap
16. Important Formulas Summary

content.label: 🎓 Exam Tips
content.text: • For exothermic reactions: Low T favors products • For endothermic reactions: High T favors products • High P favors side with fewer gas moles • Catalyst doesn't change K, only speeds up equilibrium • K >> 1 means reaction favors products • K << 1 means reaction favors reactants • Pure solids/liquids not included in K expression • Δn = moles of gaseous products - moles of gaseous reactants
17. Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin (K = °C + 273) • ❌ Including pure solids/liquids in K expression • ❌ Confusing Kp and Kc (remember Δn) • ❌ Assuming catalyst changes equilibrium position • ❌ Forgetting that K changes with temperature • ❌ Mixing up acidic and basic buffer components • ❌ Not balancing equation before writing K expression • ❌ Forgetting that pH + pOH = 14 only at 25°C
